FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD) data from 2016-2021, U.S. sample with baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using growth mixture modeling to identify subgroups of adolescents with similar screen use trajectories.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Preventive Medicine Reports.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use is the DV; predictors are sociodemographic only (race/ethnicity, parental education, perceived racism/discrimination). No qualifying phone/SM variable as IV paired with individual-trait-level outcome. Shao et al. characterize trajectories by demographic/contextual variables only, failing the "individual-trait-level on the other side" requirement.

notes: Paper examines screen use trajectories over time but only analyzes associations with demographic and contextual predictors, not with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD data (11,815 children at baseline, ages 9-10) across 22 U.S. sites. Also independently validates with HCP-D sample.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns, with cross-sectional and longitudinal associations to behavioral/brain outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Qualifies on multiple estimates. SMA pattern (video-centric vs. lower-frequency) paired with individual-trait-level outcomes: β = -0.12 (neurocognitive performance), β = 0.13 (behavioral problems), β = 0.31 (psychotic-like experiences), higher impulsivity, altered RSFC across multiple brain networks. Each outcome is individual-trait-level (behavioral, cognitive, neural, personality-related).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: standardized β coefficients for neurocognitive composite (β = -0.12, 95% CI [-0.16, -0.08], FDR-corrected p < 0.001), total behavioral problems (β = 0.13, 95% CI [0.09, 0.18], FDR-corrected p < 0.001), psychotic-like experiences (β = 0.31, 95% CI [0.27, 0.36], FDR-corrected p < 0.001), RSFC coefficients across 38 significant features.

FT_IC4.evidence: Paper reports quantitative results including standardized regression coefficients (β), confidence intervals, and FDR-corrected p-values for all major associations. Results persist in longitudinal analyses.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper contains cross-sectional analyses with SMA patterns as (effectively) predictor of behavioral/cognitive outcomes. Cross-sectional carve-out applies: even though longitudinal analyses are also present, presence of any cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis disqualifies FT-EC7.
notes: Strong paper meeting all criteria. SMA patterns identified via unsupervised clustering; associations with behavioral, cognitive, neuroimaging outcomes examined both cross-sectionally and longitudinally. Video-centric higher-frequency SMA pattern shows poorer developmental outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data (11,530 at baseline, 10,933 at 1-year follow-up; ages 9-10 baseline) across 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis developing and testing cross-validated machine learning models (PCR and LASSO) for predicting ADHD symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Translational Psychiatry.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time appears as one feature in a machine learning model predicting ADHD symptoms, but it is not analyzed as a standalone IV in an inferential association with a single outcome. Screen time is a covariate/feature in a multi-domain predictive model (alongside neurocognition, demographics, impulsivity, parental monitoring). The paper does not report a direct coefficient for screen time ↔ ADHD relationship; screen time's contribution is embedded within the feature importance weights of the larger predictive framework. This fails FT-IC2's requirement for a qualifying phone/SM variable paired with an individual-trait outcome in an inferential analysis with its own reported estimate.

notes: Paper is a machine learning prediction study; screen time is included as a feature but not as a standalone predictor variable with its own reported association coefficient. This is fundamentally different from reporting an inferential estimate (β, OR, r) for screen time as an IV or DV.
